records.ety: [OE. crok; akin to Icel. krōkr hook, bend, SW. krok, Dan. krog, OD. krooke; or cf. Gael. crocan crook, hook, W. crwca crooked. Cf. Crosier, Crotchet, Crutch, Encroach.]

defs: A bend, turn, or curve; curvature; flexure.
quotes: Through lanes, and crooks, and darkness.

defs: Any implement having a bent or crooked end.
The staff used by a shepherd, the hook of which serves to hold a runaway sheep.
A bishop's staff of office. Cf. Pastoral staff.
quotes: He left his crook, he left his flocks.

defs: An artifice; trick; tricky device; subterfuge.
quotes: For all yuor brags, hooks, and crooks.

defs: A small tube, usually curved, applied to a trumpet, horn, etc., to change its pitch or key.
A person given to fraudulent practices; an accomplice of thieves, forgers, etc.

records.ety: [OE. croken; cf. Sw. kr<?/ka, Dan. kr<?/ge. See Crook, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Crooked (kr??kt); p. pr. & vb. n. Crooking.]

defs: To turn from a straight line; to bend; to curve.
quotes: Crook the pregnant hinges of the knee.

defs: To turn from the path of rectitude; to pervert; to misapply; to twist.
quotes: There is no one thing that crooks youth more than such unlawfull games.
What soever affairs pass such a man's hands, he crooketh them to his own ends.

defs: To bend; to curve; to wind; to have a curvature.
quotes: Their shoes and pattens are snouted, and piked more than a finger long, crooking upwards.
